concepts.name: Structure of Carbonyl Group
concepts.explanation: The carbonyl carbon is spآ² hybridised, forming three sigma bonds (120آ° bond angles) and one pi bond with oxygen. The C=O bond is polarised due to oxygen's higher electronegativity, making the carbonyl carbon electrophilic and the carbonyl oxygen nucleophilic.

concepts.name: Nomenclature of Aldehydes â€“ IUPAC and Common
concepts.explanation: IUPAC: Replace '-e' of alkane with '-al'. The carbon of the aldehyde group is numbered as C-1. Common names are derived from corresponding carboxylic acids by replacing '-ic acid' with '-aldehyde'. Examples: Methanal (formaldehyde), Ethanal (acetaldehyde).

concepts.name: Nomenclature of Ketones â€“ IUPAC and Common
concepts.explanation: IUPAC: Replace '-e' of alkane with '-one'. Numbering begins from the end nearer to the carbonyl group. Common names: name the two alkyl/aryl groups attached to carbonyl followed by 'ketone'. Examples: Propanone (acetone), Butan-2-one (ethyl methyl ketone).

concepts.name: Preparation of Aldehydes â€“ Oxidation of Alcohols
concepts.explanation: Primary alcohols are oxidised to aldehydes using mild oxidising agents like PCC (pyridinium chlorochromate) or CrOâ‚ƒ in anhydrous medium. Example: CHâ‚ƒCHâ‚‚OH â†’ CHâ‚ƒCHO (using PCC).

concepts.name: Preparation of Aldehydes â€“ Dehydrogenation of Alcohols
concepts.explanation: Alcohol vapours are passed over heated copper or silver catalysts (573 K). Primary alcohols give aldehydes, secondary alcohols give ketones. Example: CHâ‚ƒCHâ‚‚OH â†’ CHâ‚ƒCHO + Hâ‚‚ (Cu/573K).

concepts.name: Preparation of Aldehydes â€“ Ozonolysis of Alkenes
concepts.explanation: Ozonolysis of alkenes followed by reductive workup (Zn/Hâ‚‚O) gives aldehydes, ketones, or a mixture. The position of the double bond determines the carbonyl products.

concepts.name: Preparation of Aldehydes â€“ Hydration of Alkynes
concepts.explanation: Ethyne (acetylene) on hydration in presence of Hâ‚‚SOâ‚„ and HgSOâ‚„ gives acetaldehyde. Other alkynes give ketones. Example: HCâ‰،CH + Hâ‚‚O â†’ CHâ‚ƒCHO (HgSOâ‚„/Hâ‚‚SOâ‚„).

concepts.name: Preparation of Aldehydes â€“ Rosenmund Reduction
concepts.explanation: Acyl chlorides (acid chlorides) are hydrogenated over palladium on barium sulphate (Pd/BaSOâ‚„) to give aldehydes. The catalyst is poisoned to prevent further reduction to alcohols. Example: RCOCl + Hâ‚‚ â†’ RCHO + HCl (Pd/BaSOâ‚„).

concepts.name: Preparation of Aldehydes â€“ Stephen Reaction
concepts.explanation: Nitriles are reduced to imines with stannous chloride (SnClâ‚‚) and HCl, which on hydrolysis give aldehydes. Example: RCN + SnClâ‚‚/HCl â†’ RCH=NH â†’ RCHO + NHâ‚ƒ.

concepts.name: Preparation of Aldehydes â€“ From Esters using DIBAL-H
concepts.explanation: Esters are reduced to aldehydes using diisobutylaluminium hydride (DIBAL-H) followed by hydrolysis. Example: RCOOR' + DIBAL-H â†’ RCHO + R'OH.

concepts.name: Preparation of Aromatic Aldehydes â€“ Etard Reaction
concepts.explanation: Toluene is oxidised with chromyl chloride (CrOâ‚‚Clâ‚‚) to a chromium complex, which on hydrolysis gives benzaldehyde. Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚…CHâ‚ƒ + CrOâ‚‚Clâ‚‚ â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…CHO.

concepts.name: Preparation of Aromatic Aldehydes â€“ Chromic Oxide/Acetic Anhydride Method
concepts.explanation: Toluene is treated with chromic oxide (CrOâ‚ƒ) in acetic anhydride to form benzylidene diacetate, which is hydrolysed to benzaldehyde. Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚…CHâ‚ƒ â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…CH(OCOCHâ‚ƒ)â‚‚ â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…CHO.

concepts.name: Preparation of Aromatic Aldehydes â€“ Side Chain Chlorination and Hydrolysis
concepts.explanation: Toluene is chlorinated to benzal chloride (Câ‚†Hâ‚…CHClâ‚‚), which on hydrolysis gives benzaldehyde. This is a commercial method. Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚…CHâ‚ƒ + 2Clâ‚‚ â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…CHClâ‚‚ â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…CHO.

concepts.name: Preparation of Aromatic Aldehydes â€“ Gatterman-Koch Reaction
concepts.explanation: Benzene or substituted benzene reacts with CO and HCl in presence of anhydrous AlClâ‚ƒ and CuCl to give benzaldehyde or substituted benzaldehyde. Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚† + CO + HCl â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…CHO (AlClâ‚ƒ/CuCl).

concepts.name: Preparation of Ketones â€“ From Acyl Chlorides and Dialkylcadmium
concepts.explanation: Acyl chlorides react with dialkylcadmium (prepared from Grignard reagent and CdClâ‚‚) to give ketones. Example: 2RCOCl + R'â‚‚Cd â†’ 2RCOR' + CdClâ‚‚.

concepts.name: Preparation of Ketones â€“ From Nitriles and Grignard Reagents
concepts.explanation: Nitriles react with Grignard reagents followed by hydrolysis to give ketones. Example: RCN + R'MgX â†’ R-C(=NH)-R' â†’ R-CO-R' + NHâ‚ƒ.

concepts.name: Preparation of Ketones â€“ Friedel-Crafts Acylation
concepts.explanation: Benzene reacts with acyl chloride in presence of anhydrous AlClâ‚ƒ to give aromatic ketones. Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚† + CHâ‚ƒCOCl â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…COCHâ‚ƒ + HCl (AlClâ‚ƒ).

concepts.name: Physical Properties of Aldehydes and Ketones
concepts.explanation: Formaldehyde is a gas; other lower members are volatile liquids. Boiling points are higher than hydrocarbons and ethers (due to dipole-dipole interactions) but lower than alcohols (no H-bonding). Lower members are miscible with water due to H-bonding.

concepts.name: Nucleophilic Addition Reactions â€“ Mechanism
concepts.explanation: A nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon from a direction perpendicular to the plane. The carbon changes from spآ² to spآ³, forming a tetrahedral alkoxide intermediate, which is protonated to give the addition product.

concepts.name: Reactivity of Aldehydes vs Ketones in Nucleophilic Addition
concepts.explanation: Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones due to: (i) steric reasons â€“ two bulky groups in ketones hinder nucleophile approach; (ii) electronic reasons â€“ two alkyl groups in ketones reduce electrophilicity of carbonyl carbon more effectively.

concepts.name: Nucleophilic Addition â€“ Addition of HCN (Cyanohydrin Formation)
concepts.explanation: Aldehydes and ketones react with HCN (catalysed by base) to form cyanohydrins. Example: CHâ‚ƒCHO + HCN â†’ CHâ‚ƒCH(OH)CN (cyanohydrin). Cyanohydrins are useful synthetic intermediates.

concepts.name: Nucleophilic Addition â€“ Addition of NaHSOâ‚ƒ
concepts.explanation: Aldehydes and methyl ketones react with sodium bisulphite to form crystalline bisulphite addition products. These are water-soluble and can be hydrolysed back to the carbonyl compound. Used for purification.

concepts.name: Nucleophilic Addition â€“ Addition of Alcohols (Acetal Formation)
concepts.explanation: Aldehydes react with one equivalent of alcohol to form hemiacetals, and with excess alcohol in presence of dry HCl to form acetals (gem-dialkoxy compounds). Ketones form ketals with diols (e.g., ethylene glycol).

concepts.name: Nucleophilic Addition â€“ Addition of Ammonia Derivatives
concepts.explanation: Aldehydes and ketones react with NHâ‚ƒ and its derivatives (Hâ‚‚N-Z) to form imines or derivatives. Examples: hydroxylamine (NHâ‚‚OH) gives oximes; hydrazine (NHâ‚‚NHâ‚‚) gives hydrazones; 2,4-DNP gives 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazones (yellow/orange solids).

concepts.name: Reduction of Aldehydes and Ketones to Alcohols
concepts.explanation: Aldehydes reduce to primary alcohols; ketones reduce to secondary alcohols. Reagents: NaBHâ‚„, LiAlHâ‚„, or catalytic hydrogenation (Hâ‚‚/Ni, Pt, or Pd). Example: CHâ‚ƒCHO â†’ CHâ‚ƒCHâ‚‚OH.
concepts.formula_reaction: RCHO â†’ RCHâ‚‚OH; RCOR' â†’ RCH(OH)R'

concepts.name: Reduction of Carbonyl Group to Methylene (Clemmensen and Wolff-Kishner)
concepts.explanation: Clemmensen reduction: >C=O â†’ >CHâ‚‚ using Zn-Hg and conc. HCl. Wolff-Kishner reduction: >C=O â†’ >CHâ‚‚ using NHâ‚‚NHâ‚‚ followed by KOH/ethylene glycol. Example: RCHO â†’ RCHâ‚ƒ.

concepts.name: Oxidation of Aldehydes
concepts.explanation: Aldehydes are easily oxidised to carboxylic acids by mild oxidising agents like Tollens' reagent, Fehling's reagent, or KMnOâ‚„. Ketones require strong oxidising conditions and undergo C-C bond cleavage.

concepts.name: Tollens' Test (Silver Mirror Test)
concepts.explanation: Aldehydes reduce ammoniacal silver nitrate (Tollens' reagent) to metallic silver, forming a bright silver mirror. Aromatic aldehydes also give this test. Ketones do not react. Used to distinguish aldehydes from ketones.

concepts.name: Fehling's Test
concepts.explanation: Aldehydes reduce Fehling's reagent (alkaline Cuآ²âپ؛ tartrate complex) to red-brown Cuâ‚‚O precipitate. Aromatic aldehydes do not respond to this test. Ketones do not react. Used to distinguish aldehydes from ketones.

concepts.name: Haloform Reaction â€“ Iodoform Test
concepts.explanation: Methyl ketones (R-CO-CHâ‚ƒ) are oxidised by sodium hypohalite (NaOX) to sodium salts of carboxylic acids with one less carbon and haloform (CHXâ‚ƒ). Iodoform test (yellow precipitate of CHIâ‚ƒ) identifies methyl ketones and compounds oxidisable to methyl ketones.

concepts.name: Acidity of خ±-Hydrogens â€“ Aldol Condensation
concepts.explanation: Aldehydes and ketones with خ±-hydrogens undergo aldol condensation in presence of dilute alkali. The خ±-H is acidic due to resonance stabilisation of the enolate. Aldehydes give خ²-hydroxy aldehydes (aldols), which dehydrate to خ±,خ²-unsaturated carbonyl compounds.

concepts.name: Cross Aldol Condensation
concepts.explanation: When two different aldehydes or ketones are used, a crossed aldol reaction occurs. To get a single product, one carbonyl should lack خ±-hydrogens (e.g., benzaldehyde, formaldehyde). Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚…CHO + CHâ‚ƒCHO â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…CH=CHCHO (cinnamaldehyde).

concepts.name: Cannizzaro Reaction
concepts.explanation: Aldehydes without خ±-hydrogens (e.g., benzaldehyde, formaldehyde) undergo disproportionation with concentrated alkali. One molecule is reduced to alcohol, the other is oxidised to carboxylate salt. Example: 2Câ‚†Hâ‚…CHO â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…CHâ‚‚OH + Câ‚†Hâ‚…COONa.

concepts.name: Crossed Cannizzaro Reaction
concepts.explanation: When a non-enolizable aldehyde is treated with formaldehyde (hydride donor), the hydride transfer occurs preferentially from formaldehyde, yielding the corresponding alcohol of the other aldehyde. Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚…CHO + HCHO â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…CHâ‚‚OH + HCOONa.
concepts.formula_reaction: Câ‚†Hâ‚…CHO + HCHO + NaOH â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…CHâ‚‚OH + HCOONa

concepts.name: Structure of Carboxyl Group
concepts.explanation: The carboxyl carbon is spآ² hybridised with bond angles ~120آ°. The C=O bond is polarised, and the C-OH bond has partial double bond character due to resonance. The carboxylate ion is stabilised by two equivalent resonance structures.

concepts.name: Nomenclature of Carboxylic Acids
concepts.explanation: IUPAC: Replace '-e' of alkane with '-oic acid'. The carboxyl carbon is numbered C-1. Common names are derived from Latin/Greek sources (e.g., formic acid from 'formica' â€“ ant, acetic acid from 'acetum' â€“ vinegar).

concepts.name: Preparation of Carboxylic Acids â€“ Oxidation of Primary Alcohols and Aldehydes
concepts.explanation: Primary alcohols are oxidised to carboxylic acids using KMnOâ‚„ (neutral/acidic/alkaline), Kâ‚‚Crâ‚‚Oâ‚‡, or CrOâ‚ƒ (Jones reagent). Aldehydes are also oxidised by mild oxidising agents. Example: RCHâ‚‚OH â†’ RCOOH.
concepts.formula_reaction: RCHâ‚‚OH + [O] â†’ RCOOH; RCHO + [O] â†’ RCOOH

concepts.name: Preparation of Carboxylic Acids â€“ Oxidation of Alkylbenzenes
concepts.explanation: Alkylbenzenes are oxidised to aromatic carboxylic acids by strong oxidising agents (KMnOâ‚„ or Kâ‚‚Crâ‚‚Oâ‚‡). The entire side chain is oxidised to COOH, irrespective of chain length. Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚…CHâ‚ƒ â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…COOH.
concepts.formula_reaction: Câ‚†Hâ‚…R + [O] â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…COOH + R'COOH (if R is primary/secondary)

concepts.name: Preparation of Carboxylic Acids â€“ Hydrolysis of Nitriles and Amides
concepts.explanation: Nitriles are hydrolysed to amides and then to carboxylic acids in presence of Hâپ؛ or OHâپ». This method increases the carbon chain by one atom. Example: RCN + 2Hâ‚‚O â†’ RCOOH + NHâ‚ƒ (Hâپ؛ or OHâپ»).

concepts.name: Preparation of Carboxylic Acids â€“ From Grignard Reagents and COâ‚‚
concepts.explanation: Grignard reagents react with COâ‚‚ (dry ice) to give carboxylate salts, which on acidification give carboxylic acids. This method also increases the carbon chain by one atom. Example: RMgX + COâ‚‚ â†’ RCOOMgX â†’ RCOOH.

concepts.name: Physical Properties of Carboxylic Acids
concepts.explanation: Carboxylic acids have higher boiling points than aldehydes, ketones, and even alcohols of comparable molecular mass due to extensive intermolecular hydrogen bonding (dimer formation in vapour phase). Lower members (up to Câ‚„) are miscible with water.

concepts.name: Acidity of Carboxylic Acids
concepts.explanation: Carboxylic acids are weak acids but stronger than alcohols and phenols. The carboxylate ion is stabilised by resonance (negative charge delocalised over two oxygen atoms). pKa values: acetic acid (4.76), benzoic acid (4.19), formic acid (3.77).
concepts.formula_reaction: RCOOH + Hâ‚‚O â‡Œ RCOOâپ» + Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛; pKa = -log Ka

concepts.name: Effect of Substituents on Acidity of Carboxylic Acids
concepts.explanation: Electron-withdrawing groups (EWG) increase acidity by stabilising the carboxylate ion (e.g., ClCHâ‚‚COOH > CHâ‚ƒCOOH). Electron-donating groups (EDG) decrease acidity. The effect is stronger at ortho and para positions in aromatic acids.

concepts.name: Reactions with Metals, Carbonates, and Bicarbonates
concepts.explanation: Carboxylic acids react with active metals (Na, K) to evolve Hâ‚‚, and with carbonates/bicarbonates to evolve COâ‚‚. This reaction distinguishes carboxylic acids from phenols and alcohols. Example: RCOOH + NaHCOâ‚ƒ â†’ RCOONa + Hâ‚‚O + COâ‚‚.
concepts.formula_reaction: RCOOH + NaHCOâ‚ƒ â†’ RCOONa + Hâ‚‚O + COâ‚‚

concepts.name: Esterification of Carboxylic Acids (Fischer Esterification)
concepts.explanation: Carboxylic acids react with alcohols in presence of acid catalyst (conc. Hâ‚‚SOâ‚„ or HCl) to form esters. The reaction is reversible and proceeds via nucleophilic acyl substitution. Example: CHâ‚ƒCOOH + Câ‚‚Hâ‚…OH â†’ CHâ‚ƒCOOCâ‚‚Hâ‚… + Hâ‚‚O.

concepts.name: Formation of Acid Chlorides (Acyl Chlorides)
concepts.explanation: Carboxylic acids react with PClâ‚…, PClâ‚ƒ, or SOClâ‚‚ to give acid chlorides. SOClâ‚‚ is preferred as the by-products (SOâ‚‚, HCl) are gaseous and escape. Example: RCOOH + SOClâ‚‚ â†’ RCOCl + SOâ‚‚ + HCl.
concepts.formula_reaction: RCOOH + SOClâ‚‚ â†’ RCOCl + SOâ‚‚ + HCl

concepts.name: Formation of Amides
concepts.explanation: Carboxylic acids react with ammonia to form ammonium salts, which on heating give amides. Example: RCOOH + NHâ‚ƒ â†’ RCOONHâ‚„ â†’ RCONHâ‚‚ + Hâ‚‚O.

concepts.name: Reduction of Carboxylic Acids to Primary Alcohols
concepts.explanation: Carboxylic acids are reduced to primary alcohols by LiAlHâ‚„ or diborane (Bâ‚‚Hâ‚†). NaBHâ‚„ does not reduce carboxylic acids. Example: RCOOH + LiAlHâ‚„ â†’ RCHâ‚‚OH.
concepts.formula_reaction: RCOOH + LiAlHâ‚„ â†’ RCHâ‚‚OH + Hâ‚‚O (acid workup)

concepts.name: Decarboxylation of Carboxylic Acids
concepts.explanation: Sodium salts of carboxylic acids on heating with soda lime (NaOH + CaO) give alkanes with one carbon less (decarboxylation). Example: CHâ‚ƒCOONa + NaOH â†’ CHâ‚„ + Naâ‚‚COâ‚ƒ.
concepts.formula_reaction: RCOONa + NaOH â†’ RH + Naâ‚‚COâ‚ƒ (CaO, heat)

concepts.name: Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky (HVZ) Reaction
concepts.explanation: Carboxylic acids with خ±-hydrogens are halogenated at the خ±-position using Clâ‚‚ or Brâ‚‚ in presence of red phosphorus (catalytic). The خ±-halo acid is formed. Example: CHâ‚ƒCOOH + Brâ‚‚ + P â†’ CHâ‚‚BrCOOH.
concepts.formula_reaction: RCHâ‚‚COOH + Xâ‚‚ + P â†’ RCH(X)COOH + HX

concepts.name: Electrophilic Substitution in Aromatic Carboxylic Acids
concepts.explanation: The carboxyl group is deactivating and meta-directing in electrophilic aromatic substitution. Aromatic carboxylic acids undergo nitration, halogenation, etc., but do not undergo Friedel-Crafts reactions (carboxyl group deactivates the ring).

concepts.name: Uses of Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
concepts.explanation: Formaldehyde (as formalin) preserves biological specimens and makes resins. Acetaldehyde is used for acetic acid and drugs. Benzaldehyde is used in perfumery. Acetone and MEK are industrial solvents. Acetic acid is vinegar and a solvent. Benzoic acid and its salts are food preservatives.
